abraham_lincoln: Abraham Lincoln is consistently ranked as one of the greatest US presidents, but I want to understand what specifically made him so effective — what qualities or decisions earned that reputation?

I was taught that Lincoln freed the slaves, but I've read that the Emancipation Proclamation was more limited than that. Did Lincoln personally free enslaved people, and what were the actual limits of the Proclamation?
I've heard the Gettysburg Address described as one of the greatest speeches in American history, but it's quite short and I'm not sure I fully grasp what Lincoln was saying. Can you explain it?
Beyond his political achievements, what was Lincoln like as a person — his personality, habits, relationships, and private life?

The Civil War created enormous political pressures — hostile Congress, fractured party, border states to keep loyal. How did Lincoln navigate all of that and hold the Union's political coalition together?
I know John Wilkes Booth assassinated Lincoln, but I've never fully understood who Booth was, what motivated him, or whether he was part of a larger conspiracy.
I've read that Lincoln suffered from serious depression throughout his life, which was understood very differently in the 19th century. What do we actually know about his mental health?

Lincoln lived in the 1800s when ideas about race were very different from today. How do historians assess his racial views honestly — was he progressive for his time, or does he look problematic by modern standards?
Lincoln is often compared to other wartime presidents like FDR and Churchill. How does Lincoln measure up against other leaders who governed during major conflicts?

Lincoln was assassinated just days after the Civil War effectively ended, before Reconstruction had really begun. What do historians think would have happened if he had survived to lead the country through that period?
george_washington: George Washington is treated as an almost mythical figure in American history. Setting aside the hagiography, was he genuinely as capable and important as his reputation suggests, or does being first give him an unfair advantage in historical rankings?
After the Revolutionary War, Washington was enormously popular and some people reportedly wanted to make him king. Why did he refuse, and what does that decision tell us about him?
I grew up hearing the story about George Washington and a cherry tree, where he supposedly said 'I cannot tell a lie.' Is there any truth to that story, or is it entirely made up?

Washington led the Continental Army through a difficult eight-year war against the most powerful military of the era. What kind of general was he — what were his strengths and biggest mistakes in the field?
I know Washington owned enslaved people, which creates obvious tension with his legacy as a founder of a nation built on liberty. What's the full story of Washington and slavery?
Washington served only two terms as president and then voluntarily stepped down, which was considered remarkable at the time. Was that his personal choice, or were there other pressures involved?
Beyond his public role as general and president, what was Washington like privately — his personality, his daily routines, his relationships, and what he cared about personally?
Washington made many consequential decisions as the first president, essentially setting precedents with no guidebook to follow. Which of his presidential decisions had the most lasting impact?
There was a major domestic crisis during Washington's presidency called the Whiskey Rebellion. What happened, and why was Washington's response to it historically significant?
Both Washington and Jefferson were founding fathers from Virginia and plantation owners, but they seem to have had different philosophies and personalities. How do they compare as historical figures?
stephen_hawking: Stephen Hawking is a celebrated physicist, but beyond the popular science books and the wheelchair, I'm not sure what he specifically contributed to physics. Can you explain his most important scientific work?
Hawking radiation is Hawking's most famous theoretical contribution to physics. Can you explain what it says and why it was such a significant idea?

Hawking was diagnosed with ALS in his early twenties and was told he had only a few years to live. He survived for another 55 years. How did he manage to do world-class physics while progressively losing all physical function?
Hawking's work on black holes changed how physicists think about them. What were his most important insights specifically, and how did they change the field?
Hawking was famously reluctant to discuss traditional religion, but he did make public statements about God and the existence of a creator. What did Hawking actually believe about those questions?
Hawking's 'A Brief History of Time' became one of the best-selling popular science books ever written. Is it actually readable for someone with no physics background, and what is it really about?
Hawking was known for his sense of humor, his cameos on TV shows, and a very distinct public persona. What was he like as a person behind that public image?
Both Hawking and Einstein are considered among the greatest theoretical physicists who ever lived. How do physicists compare their contributions and their places in the history of the field?

Hawking made a series of public predictions about the future of humanity — warning about AI, alien contact, climate change, and the need to leave Earth. How accurate or well-founded were those predictions?
Despite his enormous fame and influence, Hawking never won a Nobel Prize. Why not — was he overlooked, or was there a legitimate reason he didn't qualify?
the_korean_war: I was looking at a map and noticed North and South Korea are completely separate countries — can you explain why Korea ended up divided in the first place?
I just read a quick summary of the Korean War but it left me confused about the basics. Can you walk me through what actually happened from start to finish?
I came across a reference to President Truman firing General MacArthur during the Korean War. What happened there, and why was it such a big deal?
I've heard people say that Chinese intervention basically saved North Korea from being defeated in the Korean War. Is that accurate, and what did China's involvement actually look like?
I found out that the Korean War never officially ended with a peace treaty — just an armistice. Can you explain why the war is technically still not over seventy years later?
I keep seeing references to 'the 38th parallel' when people talk about Korea, but I'm not sure what it is or why it matters. Can you explain it?
I'm studying 20th-century wars and I want to understand how the Korean War and the Vietnam War compare — different causes, different outcomes, different lessons learned?
I've seen the Korean War described as 'the forgotten war' in history books. Why does it get so much less attention than World War II or Vietnam, even though hundreds of thousands died?
I'm trying to understand how the Korean War fits into the broader Cold War between the US and the Soviet Union. Did it change anything significantly about how that rivalry played out?
I just watched a documentary that mentioned the Inchon landing as a turning point in the Korean War. Can you explain what the Inchon landing was and why it mattered?
india: India has hundreds of languages and thousands of dialects — more than almost any other country. What explains that extraordinary linguistic diversity, and how does the country hold together with so many different languages?

The caste system has shaped Indian society for millennia, and despite legal prohibitions it persists in modern India. Can you explain what the caste system is and what it looks like in practice today?

India's independence from Britain in 1947 was a complicated process involving the partition of the subcontinent into India and Pakistan. How did independence come about, and what made the partition so violent?
Hindi and Urdu are sometimes described as the same language written differently, and other times as distinct languages. What's the actual linguistic relationship between them?
Cricket isn't just popular in India — it's an obsession that generates revenues comparable to major American sports. How did a British colonial game become so central to Indian national identity?

Bollywood produces more films per year than Hollywood, and Indian cinema has fans all over the world outside traditional Western markets. What's the history of Bollywood and why has its appeal spread so far?
India is the world's largest democracy, with over a billion eligible voters. How does Indian democracy actually work — the parliamentary system, the parties, the elections?

Arranged marriages are still common in India across all regions and many social classes, though the practice has changed significantly. What do arranged marriages actually look like in contemporary India?

The Hindu pantheon has millions of gods and goddesses, which can be bewildering from the outside. How does Hinduism understand its enormous number of deities — are they all separate, or expressions of something unified?

India has produced globally dominant technology companies and a massive software services industry, with major cities like Bangalore becoming tech hubs. How did the Indian technology industry become so significant so quickly?
italy: I've always been fascinated by how different Italian sounds from region to region — why does Italy have so many distinct regional dialects when the country isn't that large?
I studied the Roman Empire briefly in school but I'd love a more honest answer: how did such a powerful empire actually fall? Was it really just one thing or a slow collapse over centuries?
I keep seeing references to the Italian mafia in films and news but I'm not sure how much is myth and how much is real — what's the actual story of organized crime in Italy?
One thing that strikes me every time I visit Italian friends is how central family is to everything they do. Where does that strong family culture come from historically?
I know the Renaissance was a huge deal but I've never really understood why it started in Italy specifically. Can you explain the Renaissance and what made Italy the center of it?
Italy seems to change governments constantly compared to other European democracies. Why is Italian politics so unstable, and is it always been that way?
I'm planning a trip to Italy and keep hearing that the north and south are basically two different countries. What are the real differences between northern and southern Italy?
The Vatican is physically inside Rome but operates as its own sovereign country. How did that arrangement come about, and why is the Vatican its own independent state?
Italy was a patchwork of city-states and kingdoms for centuries — how did it eventually become a single unified nation, and when did that actually happen?
Italian brands like Gucci, Ferrari, and Armani seem to dominate global luxury and design. How did Italy develop such an outsized influence on fashion and design worldwide?
basketball_nba: The GOAT debate in basketball always comes down to Michael Jordan and LeBron James, and it never seems to get resolved. Who actually has the stronger case based on achievements, competition, and impact?
The NBA has fans and star players from every continent now, which wasn't true thirty years ago. How did basketball become such a global sport when it used to feel so distinctly American?
Basketball was invented in a Massachusetts YMCA in 1891 and is now one of the most watched sports on earth. How did it grow from a gym class invention to a global phenomenon?
NBA players make extraordinary salaries — some over fifty million dollars a year. How did player salaries get this high, and how do the salary cap and max contract rules actually work?
Basketball used to be dominated by big men in the paint, but now teams shoot constant three-pointers. How has the three-point shot changed NBA strategy, and is the game actually better for it?
NBA coaches can make or break a team's culture, but their roles aren't always well understood. What does a great NBA head coach actually do — what's the job beyond drawing up plays?
NBA trades happen frequently and the mechanics seem complicated — sign-and-trades, buyouts, draft pick compensation. How do NBA trades actually work, and what constraints do teams operate under?
The Western Conference has historically produced more elite teams and tougher playoff matchups than the East. Is the West really that much harder, and why has that imbalance persisted for so long?
The NBA playoffs are a two-month marathon involving sixteen teams. Can you walk me through how the NBA playoff format works from start to the Finals?
Stephen Curry transformed how teams think about shooting and spacing in ways that seem permanent. How exactly did Curry change basketball, and why has his impact been so lasting?
the_brain: I've heard the claim that humans use only 10% of their brain repeated so many times that I'm not sure if it's true. Is it, and if not, where did that idea come from?
I know my brain stores memories, but the mechanism has always seemed mysterious to me. How does memory actually work at the biological level — where and how are memories stored?
The left brain/right brain theory — creative right brain, logical left brain — seems to be everywhere in popular psychology. Is it scientifically accurate, or is it largely a myth?

Consciousness is something I experience every moment of my life, but I've read that neuroscientists have no consensus explanation for what it is. What is consciousness made of, and why is it such a hard problem?
I've heard that learning new skills or information physically changes the brain. How does learning actually alter brain structure and function?

People often compare the brain to a computer, but I've also heard neuroscientists say that comparison is deeply misleading. How accurate is the brain-as-computer analogy, and where does it break down?
I know sleep is important, but I've never understood what my brain is actually doing while I'm asleep. What happens in the brain during sleep, and why is it necessary?
The prefrontal cortex is the part of the brain most associated with decision-making and self-control, and I've heard it develops relatively late in life. What does the prefrontal cortex actually do?
I've always assumed that the brain cells you're born with are all you get — that adult brains don't generate new neurons. Is that true, or can the adult brain grow new cells?

Neuroscience has advanced enormously in recent decades, but there are still fundamental questions about the brain that remain unanswered. What are the biggest open mysteries that brain science hasn't solved yet?
game_of_thrones: Game of Thrones season 8 was one of the most controversial television endings in history. Having had a few years to reflect on it, is the ending actually as bad as the immediate backlash suggested?
Game of Thrones has one of the largest and most memorable casts in television history. Who is your absolute favorite character across the entire series and why?
House of the Dragon is the Game of Thrones prequel series set about 170 years before the original. Do you think it's actually better than Game of Thrones at its peak?
Game of Thrones had enormous quality variation across its eight seasons. Which season do you think is the absolute best, and which is the weakest?
Given the divisive ending and years of discourse about the show's decline, is Game of Thrones still worth rewatching in 2026, or has it been tainted by how it ended?

Daenerys Targaryen's turn to burning King's Landing is one of the most debated character moments in television history. Was her heel turn into the Mad Queen a justified character arc, or was it too rushed to be believable?
Game of Thrones produced dozens of landmark television episodes. What is your single favorite episode in the entire series and what makes it the best?
Game of Thrones deliberately subverted expectations about who should sit on the Iron Throne. Looking back at how it ended, who do you think actually should have ended up ruling Westeros?
The Red Wedding in Game of Thrones season 3 is one of the most shocking moments in television history. Is it also the best single scene in the show, or does something else in the series top it?

George R.R. Martin still hasn't finished The Winds of Winter, the next book in the A Song of Ice and Fire series. Should a Game of Thrones fan bother reading the books given they may never be completed?
harry_potter: I've read the Harry Potter series twice now and I'm curious what other fans think — which book in the series do you think is the strongest, and why?
I've always gone back and forth on this: is Severus Snape ultimately a hero or a villain in the Harry Potter series? He does terrible things but also sacrifices everything.

I'm taking a Harry Potter sorting quiz and I keep getting different results. If you had to sort someone who loves reading, values knowledge, but absolutely hates following rules, which Hogwarts house fits best?
I just finished rereading Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows and I'm still puzzled — why did Dumbledore trust Snape so completely for all those years? What was the basis of that trust?
A friend is getting into Harry Potter for the first time. Should they read the books before watching the films, or does it not matter which order they start with?
I want to revisit the Harry Potter film series but I can't remember which ones I liked. Can you rank all eight movies from worst to best and explain your reasoning?
Is Harry Potter and the Cursed Child considered official canon in the Harry Potter universe, or do most fans treat it as a separate thing?
Who is your favorite character in the Harry Potter series and what makes them stand out to you compared to the rest of the cast?
My little cousin just started getting into Harry Potter and asked me what a horcrux is. Can you explain horcruxes in really simple terms, like you're talking to a young kid?
Now that I know Harry is a horcrux, I want to know — was that actually foreshadowed earlier in the Harry Potter series, or does it feel like it was made up later?
